depth.text: tpu
A TPU (Tensor Processing Unit) is Google's custom ASIC for accelerating ML workloads, built around matrix-multiply units (MXUs) inside TensorCores.
TPU v5p delivers 459 BF16 TFLOPS per chip with 95 GiB of HBM at 2,575 GiBps bandwidth; its pod connects 8,960 chips over a 3D torus.

Across generations the per-chip BF16 peak has grown from 275 TFLOPS (v4) to 459 TFLOPS (v5p) to 918 TFLOPS (v6e Trillium) to 2,307 TFLOPS (v7 Ironwood)-roughly 8x in three generations. HBM per chip grew from 32 GB (v4) to 192 GiB (v7), while the pod roofline ridge point for v5e sits at ~240 FLOPs/byte, meaning matrix multiplications must exceed that arithmetic intensity to be compute-bound rather than memory-bound.
tensorcore
A TensorCore is the main compute block inside a TPU chip, containing matrix-multiply units (MXUs), a vector unit, and a scalar unit.
TPU v5e and v6e each have one TensorCore per chip; TPU v4, v5p, and v7 Ironwood each have two TensorCores per chip.

Trillium (v6e) delivers a 4.7x compute-per-chip gain over v5e-Google's measured figure-driven by larger MXUs and a higher clock rate. v7 Ironwood pairs two TensorCores with 4 SparseCores per chip, adding structured-sparsity acceleration for embedding workloads on top of the dense matrix compute.
torus
TPU pods use a torus interconnect-a wrap-around mesh where each chip connects to its nearest neighbors-enabling all-reduce collective operations with bounded latency.

TPU v4 and v5p use a 3D torus (each chip has 6 ICI links to neighbors); v5e and v6e use a 2D torus (4 links). The v4 pod forms a 16x16x16 cube of 4,096 chips; the v5p pod is a 16x20x28 cube of 8,960 chips.

The 3D torus of v5p directly enables a far larger pod-8,960 chips versus v5e's 256-chip 2D-torus pod. v7 Ironwood returns to a 3D torus (topology 4x4x576) with 1,200 GBps bidi ICI per chip and scales to 9,216 chips; Optical Circuit Switches (OCS) reconfigure inter-cube links dynamically, allowing the pod to tolerate chip failures and rebalance topology without rewiring.
pod
A TPU pod is the largest single-job scheduling unit: a fully interconnected set of TPU chips sharing a high-bandwidth ICI fabric.

Pod sizes differ by generation: v4 = 4,096 chips (16x16x16 3D torus); v5e = 256 chips (16x16 2D torus); v5p = 8,960 chips (16x20x28 3D torus); v6e Trillium = 256 chips (16x16 2D torus); v7 Ironwood = 9,216 chips (3D torus).

A full v4 pod delivers 1.1 ExaFLOPS BF16 across 4,096 chips; a v7 Ironwood pod delivers ~42.5 ExaFLOPS FP8 across 9,216 chips-about 38x more peak pod throughput, though across different dtypes (v4 BF16 vs v7 FP8). The v5p pod's 8,960-chip count gives 4x more total FLOPs than a v4 pod for large-scale LLM training, with 2.8x faster LLM training throughput measured by Google.
trillium
Trillium is Google's sixth-generation TPU (v6e), announced in 2024, optimized for transformer training and inference.
Each Trillium chip delivers 918 BF16 TFLOPS and 1,836 INT8 TOPS, with 32 GB HBM at 1,638 GiBps bandwidth-4.7x the compute and 2x the HBM capacity of v5e-in a 256-chip pod over a 2D torus.

Trillium achieves its 4.7x compute gain over v5e primarily through larger MXU arrays and a higher clock rate, while keeping the same 256-chip 2D-torus pod topology as v5e. At pod scale, 256 Trillium chips deliver 234.9 PetaFLOPS BF16-versus v5e's 50.6 PetaFLOPS-and Trillium is over 67% more energy-efficient per FLOP than v5e.
ironwood
Ironwood is Google's seventh-generation TPU (v7), purpose-built for the inference era, introduced in 2025.

Each Ironwood chip delivers 2,307 BF16 TFLOPS and 4,614 FP8 TFLOPS, with 192 GiB HBM3e at 7,380 GiBps bandwidth and 1,200 GBps bidirectional ICI; a full 9,216-chip pod holds 1.77 PB of HBM and reaches ~42.5 ExaFLOPS FP8.

Ironwood is ~5x the peak compute of v5p per chip (2,307 vs 459 BF16 TFLOPS; Google cites ~10x at the pod/system level) and ~2.5x the raw BF16 of Trillium per chip (2,307 vs 918 TFLOPS; Google's 4x figure is workload-level), with HBM bandwidth growing from 2,575 GiBps (v5p) to 7,380 GiBps. Its roofline ridge point is 2,307 TFLOPS / 7,380 GB/s ~ 313 FLOPs/byte-higher than v5e's ~240 FLOPs/byte-meaning Ironwood demands even denser matrix operations to reach peak utilization. The two TensorCores plus four SparseCores per chip reflect its inference-era design priority.
